perturbation.original_text: 1. The confidential, proprietary and trade secret information of the Disclosing Party (hereinafter "Confidential Information") provided hereunder is any and all information, in whatever form (including electronic data) that will be disclosed to facilitate the potential transaction of business (related to the provision of SAP Services to HRM) between the Parties, including, but not limited to, portions or parts of the November 1, 2012 Agreement (including any schedules or appendices) between the Province of Nova Scotia and IBM, each Party's disclosure of intellectual property, techniques, sketches, drawings, models, inventions, know-how, processes, apparatus, equipment, algorithms, software programs, software source documents, and formulae related to the current, future and proposed products, documents and services, research, experimental work, development, design details and specifications, engineering, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, licensing, sales or service customer lists, business forecasts, sales and merchandizing, and marketing plans and information. For purposes of this Agreement confidential, proprietary and trade secret information of the Province’s SAP Service Provi der or any other Province’s Service Provider, any parent company, subsidiary or affiliate of the Disclosing party shall be considered Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The confidential, proprietary and trade secret information of the Disclosing Party (hereinafter "Confidential Information") provided hereunder is any and all information, in whatever form (including electronic data) that will be disclosed to facilitate the potential transaction of business (related to the provision of SAP Services to HRM) between the Parties. For purposes of this Agreement confidential, proprietary and trade secret information of the Province’s SAP Service Provi der or any other Province’s Service Provider, any parent company, subsidiary or affiliate of the Disclosing party shall be considered Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the detailed list of what constitutes 'Confidential Information,' the clause becomes vague. Section 9 refers to returning 'all Confidential Information', but with the omitted specifics, it's unclear what exactly must be returned or destroyed, creating an in-text contradiction. This introduces ambiguity and uncertainty regarding the scope of confidential information covered by the agreement. The omitted specific examples like 'portions or parts of the November 1, 2012 Agreement (including any schedules or appendices) between the Province of Nova Scotia and IBM' and 'each Party's disclosure of intellectual property, techniques, sketches, drawings, models, inventions, know-how, processes, apparatus, equipment, algorithms, software programs, software source documents, and formulae related to the current, future and proposed products, documents and services, research, experimental work, development, design details and specifications, engineering, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, licensing, sales or service customer lists, business forecasts, sales and merchandizing, and marketing plans and information' are now undefined, making the practical application of Section 9 inconsistent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. This Agreement will terminate on the date and time the Agreement between the Parties over the provision of SAP Services is terminated. In the event this Agreement is terminated, and the Disclosing Party so requests, the Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information which it received from the Disclosing Party along with all copies. The obligations of the Receiving Party under this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement or the return of the Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the detailed list of what constitutes 'Confidential Information,' which was previously defined. This creates a contradiction with Section 9, which requires the Receiving Party to return or destroy 'all Confidential Information'. The lack of a clear definition in the changed text makes it unclear what exactly must be returned or destroyed, thereby creating a direct conflict and unenforceability since the scope is now vague. The original text specifically included examples like portions of the 'November 1, 2012 Agreement' and details about 'intellectual property' which are now missing. Therefore, the modified text creates an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The Receiving Party agrees that Confidential Information shall be used only for the purposes of facilitating the business relationship between the Parties. Additionally, subject to section 4 below, the Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall maintain the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own similar categories of confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances. The Receiving Party shall not make any copies of Confidential Information except as necessary to perform hereunder. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by such Party, its employees, agents, officials, representatives or consultants acting within the scope of their engagement or employment.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The Receiving Party agrees that Confidential Information shall be used only for the purposes of facilitating the business relationship between the Parties. Additionally, subject to section 4 below, the Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by such Party, its employees, agents, officials, representatives or consultants acting within the scope of their engagement or employment.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'The Receiving Party shall maintain the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own similar categories of confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.', it creates ambiguity around the required standard of care. Section 7 states that 'harm resulting from improper disclosure of Confidential Information may be irreparable', however, there is no baseline to determine what constitutes 'improper disclosure' without the requirement to maintain a reasonable degree of care. It creates uncertainty and potential legal disputes regarding the Receiving Party's liability in case of a confidentiality breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Each Party agrees that the harm resulting from improper disclosure of Confidential Information may be irreparable; therefore, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent or restrain breaches of any of the provisions of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or any of its employees, agents, officials, representatives or consultants, or to enforce the terms and provisions hereof, by an action instituted in a court of competent jurisdiction, which remedy or remedies are in addition to and not in substitution for any other remedy to which the Disclosing Party may be entitled at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the 'reasonable degree of care' standard. Section 7 references 'improper disclosure'. Without the 'reasonable degree of care' clause, 'improper disclosure' becomes ambiguous, creating a strong contradiction. The original text provides a baseline for determining improper disclosure, but the changed text removes it, making Section 7's enforcement unclear. Therefore, the change introduces a clear and impactful contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The Receiving Party will adhere to all Canadian export laws, rules and regulations, and will not export or re-export any technical data or products received from the Disclosing Party, nor will the receiving party export or re-export the associated products of such technical data, to any restricted country and/or to any company, individual or government entity listed in the Table of Denial Orders published from time to time by the Canadian Government (called denied persons).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The Receiving Party will adhere to all Canadian export laws, rules and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the phrase 'and will not export or re-export any technical data or products received from the Disclosing Party, nor will the receiving party export or re-export the associated products of such technical data, to any restricted country and/or to any company, individual or government entity listed in the Table of Denial Orders published from time to time by the Canadian Government (called denied persons)', the contract now implies full adherence to export laws but removes explicit restrictions on exporting confidential data to restricted entities. While generally adhering to Canadian export laws, it now lacks specific clauses preventing re-export to restricted countries/entities. If there is an instance where the Receiving Party exports the Confidential Information, and the Disclosing party sues, the Receiving Party can state that they only agreed to adhere to all Canadian export laws, and the contract does not have any clauses preventing them from re-exporting data to other countries. This creates a contradiction as adherence is stated but the details are omitted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. The Receiving Party will adhere to all Canadian export laws, rules and regulations.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the specific restrictions on exporting technical data to restricted countries or entities. The contradicted text merely restates the new modified text and does not specify the details of abiding to Canadian export law. While the Receiving Party is adhering to Canadian export laws in general, there is no specific clause preventing the re-export of data to countries or persons listed in the Table of Denial Orders. This leads to a contradiction because the new contract lacks specific restrictions that are expected in adherence of Canadian export law, and if the Receiving Party re-exports confidential data to restricted countries/entities, the Disclosing Party may not be able to sue them.

perturbation.original_text: 6. This Agreement will be effective as of the date of this Agreement, but will apply to any Confidential Information disclosed to the Receiving Party from the Disclosing Party prior to such date and will continue in perpetuity.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This Agreement will be effective as of the date of this Agreement, but will apply to any Confidential Information disclosed to the Receiving Party from the Disclosing Party prior to such date and will continue for 1 year.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'in perpetuity' to 'for 1 year', the agreement now has a defined end date. This contradicts the surviving obligations clause in Section 9, which states, 'The obligations of the Receiving Party under this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement'. Section 9 implies indefinite confidentiality, while Section 6 now limits the agreement to 1 year. This discrepancy could lead to disputes over whether confidentiality obligations continue beyond the 1-year term.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. This Agreement will terminate on the date and time the Agreement between the Parties over the provision of SAP Services is terminated. In the event this Agreement is terminated, and the Disclosing Party so requests, the Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information which it received from the Disclosing Party along with all copies. The obligations of the Receiving Party under this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement or the return of the Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement continues 'in perpetuity', while the changed text limits it to 'for 1 year'. The contradicted text in Section 9 states the obligations survive the termination of the agreement. The change from 'in perpetuity' to 'for 1 year' in Section 6 creates a direct contradiction with Section 9, as Section 9 implies the confidentiality obligations continue indefinitely, which is not possible if the agreement ends after 1 year, thus causing a strong contradiction that would cause confusion as to when the obligation end.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Title to, interest in, and all other rights of ownership to Confidential Information shall remain with the Disclosing Party. The information provided hereunder, including Confidential Information, is provided 3 strictly "as is" and without representation and warranty of any kind as to its accuracy, completeness , freedom from error, value, merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose or non-infringement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Title to, interest in, and all other rights of ownership to Confidential Information shall remain with the Disclosing Party. The information provided hereunder, including Confidential Information, is provided 3 strictly "as is".
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and without representation and warranty of any kind as to its accuracy, completeness , freedom from error, value, merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose or non-infringement.', it changes the nature of the provided information. Section 1 refers to facilitating business transactions. By omitting any warranties it could be argued that the 'confidential information' provided could not be used as a basis for SAP services, and is strictly for show. This creates a contradiction, as one section says that the confidentiality is for SAP services, while the other section says that the information is 'as is' without any value or warranties, which would make it useless for SAP services.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. The confidential, proprietary and trade secret information of the Disclosing Party (hereinafter "Confidential Information") provided hereunder is any and all information, in whatever form (including electronic data) that will be disclosed to facilitate the potential transaction of business (related to the provision of SAP Services to HRM) between the Parties. For purposes of this Agreement confidential, proprietary and trade secret information of the Province’s SAP Service Provi der or any other Province’s Service Provider, any parent company, subsidiary or affiliate of the Disclosing party shall be considered Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the Confidential Information is provided 'as is' *and* without representation or warranty, clearly disclaiming any guarantee of its accuracy, value, or fitness. The changed text omits the 'without representation and warranty' clause, significantly altering the nature of the information provided. This omission creates a contradiction because Section 1 states the information is disclosed to 'facilitate the potential transaction of business (related to the provision of SAP Services to HRM)'. By removing the warranty disclaimer, it implies the information *could* be used for SAP services, which contradicts the fact that it's provided 'as is' without any guarantees of value or fitness. Thus, if it lacks value or fitness, it cannot be used for SAP services, which fundamentally contradicts Section 1.

perturbation.original_text: 10. The Parties acknowledge and understand that all documentation and information , including Confidential Information, provided to the Province of Nova Scotia or HRM, as the case may be, pursuant to this Agreement shall be kept confidential, subject to the provisions of the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , Province of Nova Scotia, and such other laws of the Province of Nova Scotia and Canada which may apply from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. The Parties acknowledge and understand that all documentation and information , including Confidential Information, provided to the Province of Nova Scotia or HRM, as the case may be, pursuant to this Agreement shall be kept confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'subject to the provisions of the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , Province of Nova Scotia, and such other laws of the Province of Nova Scotia and Canada which may apply from time to time.', this clause now suggests absolute confidentiality. However, the legal system must apply. This creates an in-text contradiction, as it suggests all documents must be kept confidential with no exceptions, but the reality is that FIPPA still applies. It implies the agreement supersedes existing laws, which is incorrect and could lead to misinterpretations about what information truly remains confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. The Parties acknowledge and understand that all documentation and information , including Confidential Information, provided to the Province of Nova Scotia or HRM, as the case may be, pursuant to this Agreement shall be kept confidential.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause 'subject to the provisions of the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act , Province of Nova Scotia, and such other laws of the Province of Nova Scotia and Canada which may apply from time to time.' from the original text. This creates a strong contradiction because it implies absolute confidentiality, which is not legally accurate and conflicts with the implicit understanding that laws like FIPPA still apply. It changes the scope of the clause from 'confidential subject to certain laws' to 'confidential with no exceptions', therefore the changed text contradicts the contradicted text, despite them being at the same location.
